content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission
December 14,
1994
Stevan Allen
Assembly California Legislature
Subcommittee on Fairs and Expositions
State Capitol,
Room 5155
Sacramento, California
95814
Re:
Your Request for Advice
Our File No.
A-94-354
Dear Mr. Allen:
This is in response to your letter requesting confirmation of
telephone advice provided under the provisions of the Political
Reform Act (the “Act”) 1
You have been asked to attend a one—day meeting/hearing in
Palm Springs; you have been advised that you will be reimbursed by
the California Fairs Services Authority,
a joint powers authority
and a government agency, for your airfare and one night’s lodging.
The meeting pertains to work you are currently doing as a
consultant to Assemblyman Cannella and his subcommittee on Fairs
and Expositions.
You explained that the meeting is designed to
offer guidance, perspective and tools for yourself and the other
members of the Select and Steering Committee dealing with the
Division of Fairs
& Expositions’ Re—invention Pilot Project.
This letter confirms my telephone advice that since you are
doing the work in your official capacity, the amounts received by
you as reimbursement would be deemed income within the meaning of
Section 82.030(a).
However, pursuant to Section 82030(b) (2),
“income” specifically excludes reimbursement for expenses from a
state, local or federal. governmental agency.
Accordingly, since
the California Fairs Services Authority is a government agency,
reimbursement from them for. your airfare and one night’s lodging
does not constitute income.
Furthermore, since the reimbursement
is for work—related expenses,
such reimbursement from a
governmental agency, under these facts, is also not considered a
gift.
(Section 82028(a); Allen Advice Letters,
No.
A—92-364 and
1
Government Code Sections 81000-91015.
All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
428 J Street, Suite 800 •
P.O. Box 807 •
Sacramento, CA
95804-0807 •
(916) 322-5660


File No.
A—94—354
Page
2
No. A-92-364 (f).)
Therefore, the reimbursement from the
California Fairs Services Authority does not violate the
provisions of the Act.
Sincerely,
Steven G.
Churchwell
General Counsel
By:
Jeevan Ahuja
Counsel, Legal Division
SGC:JA:ak

sections.conclusion: This letter confirms my telephone advice that since you are doing the work in your official capacity, the amounts received by you as reimbursement would be deemed income within the meaning of Section 82.030(a). However, pursuant to Section 82030(b)(2), "income" specifically excludes reimbursement for expenses from a state, local or federal governmental agency. Accordingly, since the California Fairs Services Authority is a government agency, reimbursement from them for your airfare and one night's lodging does not constitute income. Furthermore, since the reimbursement is for work-related expenses, such reimbursement from a governmental agency, under these facts, is also not considered a gift. Therefore, the reimbursement from the California Fairs Services Authority does not violate the provisions of the Act.
sections.question_synthetic: Whether reimbursement from the California Fairs Services Authority for airfare and lodging for a one-day meeting constitutes income or a gift under the Political Reform Act when the recipient is attending in an official capacity as a consultant.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: Reimbursement from the California Fairs Services Authority for airfare and lodging does not constitute income or a gift under the Political Reform Act because it is an exception provided in Section 82030(b)(2) for reimbursement of expenses from governmental agencies. The reimbursement therefore does not violate the Act.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: No formal QUESTION or CONCLUSION section headers; text organized as letter. Question and conclusion inferred from letter body. Strong confidence based on clear advice structure and explicit statutory references.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Whether reimbursement from the California Fairs Services Authority for airfare and lodging for a one-day meeting constitutes income or a gift under the Political Reform Act when the recipient is attending in an official capacity as a consultant.

CONCLUSION: This letter confirms my telephone advice that since you are doing the work in your official capacity, the amounts received by you as reimbursement would be deemed income within the meaning of Section 82.030(a). However, pursuant to Section 82030(b)(2), "income" specifically excludes reimbursement for expenses from a state, local or federal governmental agency. Accordingly, since the California Fairs Services Authority is a government agency, reimbursement from them for your airfare and one night's lodging does not constitute income. Furthermore, since the reimbursement is for work-related expenses, such reimbursement from a governmental agency, under these facts, is also not considered a gift. Therefore, the reimbursement from the California Fairs Services Authority does not violate the provisions of the Act.

The meeting pertains to work you are currently doing as a consultant to Assemblyman Cannella and his subcommittee on Fairs and Expositions. You explained that the meeting is designed to offer guidance, perspective and tools for yourself and the other members of the Select and Steering Committee dealing with the Division of Fairs & Expositions’ Re—invention Pilot Project. This letter confirms my telephone advice that since you are doing the work in your official capacity, the amounts received by you as reimbursement would be deemed income within the meaning of Section 82.030(a). However, pursuant to Section 82030(b) (2), “income” specifically excludes reimbursement for expenses from a state, local or federal. governmental agency. Accordingly, since the California Fairs Services Authority is a government agency, reimbursement from them for. your airfare and one night’s lodging does not constitute income. Furthermore, since the reimbursement is for work—related expenses, such reimbursement from a governmental agency, under these facts, is also not considered a gift. (Section 82028(a); Allen Advice Letters, No. A—92-364 and 1 Government Code Sections 81000-91015. Therefore, the reimbursement from the California Fairs Services Authority does not violate the provisions of the Act. Sincerely, Steven G. embedding.summary: FPPC confirms telephone advice that reimbursement from the California Fairs Services Authority for a legislator's travel and lodging expenses does not constitute income or a gift under the Political Reform Act. The reimbursement is excluded as an exception for expenses reimbursed by governmental agencies.
